descriptions: if something unpleasant or annoying eases off, it becomes less unpleasant or annoying
to give something less effort or energy, especially when you have been working very hard
to go more slowly, especially when you are driving or running fast
examples: Ease off a bit as you go round the bend.
If she doesn’t ease off at work, she’ll make herself ill.
If the rain eases off overnight, we’ll leave in the morning.
The pain should ease off after a couple of hours.

If she doesn’t [ease] [off] at work, she’ll make herself ill.
If the rain [eases] [off] overnight, we’ll leave in the morning.
The pain should [ease] [off] after a couple of hours.

descriptions: to make someone leave a job or position without saying anything publicly
examples: Senior party sources say Liddell will be eased out during the next few weeks.
highlighted_examples: Senior party sources say Liddell will be [eased] [out] during the next few weeks.

descriptions: same as ease off
to become less severe towards someone
examples: Government forces appear to be easing up on the rebels.
Judges have been advised to ease up on jail sentences.
Our daughter arrived and he has eased up on the partying.
The government's drive for austerity has in fact eased up in the past two years.
They waited nearly four hours for the storm to ease up.

descriptions: to gradually destroy something
examples: After nearly three years of exhaustion and emotional numbness, I decide to confront the thing eating away at me and the family.

descriptions: to make someone feel more and more unhappy or worried
examples: You could see that jealousy was eating away at her.
highlighted_examples: You could see that jealousy was [eating] [away] [at] her.

descriptions: if an activity or cost eats into your time or money, it uses more of it than you intended
same as eat away
examples: The river had eaten into the bank, and part of it had collapsed.
highlighted_examples: The river had [eaten] [into] the bank, and part of it had collapsed.

descriptions: to have a meal in a restaurant instead of at home
examples: People are spending more on eating out.

descriptions: to eat all of something
to feel a negative emotion so strongly that it is difficult to think about anything else
to like something so much that you want to hear or see more
to travel a particular distance easily and steadily
to use large amounts of your available time or money
examples: Come on, eat up your broccoli.

Having children eats up a lot of a family’s income.
Paula was eaten up by guilt for days.
The press argued over the book, and the public was eating it up.

They drove on, eating up the distance between themselves and home.
They eat up the world, and every generation of makers grows more adept, and more clever at what it does.
Within a week or two of my going back she'll be telling me to eat up my nice greens.
You can have some more tomorrow, if you're a good boy and eat up all your dinner.

descriptions: to beat someone in something such as a competition or election by a small amount

descriptions: to encourage someone to do something that they should not do

descriptions: to get just enough money or food to be able to continue to exist
to make something such as money or food last as long as possible

descriptions: to start a journey by ship
